FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, nationally representative sample of 11,875 children aged 9-10 years from 21 sites across United States

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis with correlation and regression analyses examining relationships between screen time and mental health outcomes

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper in PLoS ONE

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time variable (weekday and weekend measured separately) paired with individual-trait outcomes including depression (T-score), anxiety, internalizing problems, externalizing problems, ADHD, oppositional defiance, conduct disorder, attention problems, academic performance, and peer relationships. Step 1 met: screen time is phone/SM-specific variable from questionnaire; paired with mental health/behavioral outcomes

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple regression coefficients reported throughout Tables 2-5, with standardized beta values, t-statistics, and p-values for associations between screen time and all outcome measures

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric quantitative results throughout (standardized betas, correlation coefficients, t-statistics, p-values) for SM ↔ outcome relationships

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional design; EC7 requires longitudinal design with SM as DV in every analysis
notes: Paper examines both weekday and weekend screen time separately; analyses conducted separately by sex; findings show modest associations between screen time and various behavioral/mental health outcomes

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, subsample of 67 participants (ages 11-12 years) from 4 geographically dispersed ABCD sites

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis with descriptive statistics and correlation analyses of passively sensed smartphone use data

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper in JMIR Mental Health

FT_IC2.evidence: This is primarily a methodological/validation paper. While it measures smartphone screen time (streaming, YouTube, communication, gaming, social media, SMS, reading, music, news), the analysis focuses on concordance between self-report, parent-report, and passive sensing data (r=.49 between self-report and objective). No analysis pairs smartphone/SM use with individual-trait-level variables (neural, behavioral, health, cognitive, well-being, psychiatric symptoms). Study aims to assess feasibility and acceptability of passive sensing methodology, not to examine associations with outcomes

notes: Primary contribution is demonstrating technical feasibility and accuracy of EARS app for passive smartphone monitoring in ABCD cohort; descriptive app use data provided but no outcome associations analyzed

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, large representative cross-sectional sample of 11,875 youth aged 9-11 years from 21 U.S. recruitment sites

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using negative binomial regression examining associations between movement behavior recommendations and behavioral outcomes

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper in Journal of Adolescent Health

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Paper does not isolate a phone/SM-specific estimate. Step 2: Composite test fails. Screen time variable is defined as part of 24-hour movement guideline recommendations (≤2 hours per day) and is measured as a composite including: TV watching, video watching (YouTube), video game playing, texting on cellphone/tablet/computer, visiting social media sites, and using video chat. Methods state recreation screen time includes '(1) TV watching; (2) video watching (e.g., YouTube); (3) video game playing; (4) texting on cellphone, tablet, or computer; (5) visiting social media sites; or (6) using video chat.' This composite bundles TV (non-phone/non-SM) and video games (unspecified platform, could be console or PC) with phone/SM items without separate breakout. Main analysis examines meeting all three recommendations (physical activity + screen time + sleep) as combined predictor; no separate estimate for screen time alone as phone/SM variable paired with individual trait. NOT_MET per v7 binding rule on multi-device aggregated/composite-only

notes: Paper focuses on 24-hour movement guidelines as integrated recommendation; screen time measured as one component of behavioral bundle; outcome variables are internalizing/externalizing behaviors measured by CBCL
